### seed-083

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Diversity flag:** Iris ran ahead of casting on this one and assigned Jerry as the divorced husband. I'm swapping Jerry and Noah. Noah narrates; Jerry sits on the bench. Better rotation, better character fit. Iris — this is a character name swap in your existing concepts, not a rebuild. The blocking, expressions, and scene structure all hold.

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Divorced Husband / Narrator | Noah Check — Dad persona (adapted: Exasperated Divorced Dad) | "Compliance comedy." Noah is exactly the man who received an absurd court ruling and just... accepted it. He's not bitter. He filed the paperwork. He runs his half of the pudding shop. He explains his custody arrangement the way he explains a home insurance claim — mild inconvenience, procedural fact. "We now have shared custardy" lands 40% funnier from Noah than from Jerry because Noah sounds like he's reading from the actual court document. |
| Ex-Wife (background visual anchor) | Zoe Sparks — Goofball Neighbor (adapted: Estranged Co-Owner) | Zoe on the other side of the divide. She is fine. She has thrived. Her energy (bubbly, undefeated) is the visual counterweight to Noah's resigned acceptance. She arranged her half of the shop with better signage. She found the shared custardy arrangement charming. This is deeply unfair to Noah. |
| Judge | Jerry Gold — PTA/Coach Dad (adapted: Procedural Authority) | Jerry as the judge who issued this ruling with complete bureaucratic solemnity is excellent use of his "authority with blind spots" archetype. He thought "shared custardy" was well-worded. He is proud of it. He gaveled it in without hesitation. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Noah: No ring. Slightly rumpled business casual — a man who has been through some things but is maintaining. This is not a crisis; this is just his life now.
- Zoe: Matching apron (Concept B shop) or tidy filing (Concept A courtroom). She's the one who kept the plants alive during the divorce.
- Jerry: Judicial robes. Gavel. He is performing authority and doing it well. He has no awareness that the pun is a pun.
- **Iris direction:** In your existing concept scenes, swap character assignments. Noah is now where Jerry was described (defendant's table / shop counter). Jerry is now where Noah was described (judge's bench / elevated position). Blocking, expressions, and prop notes all apply to the swapped characters.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Noah to camera, mid-exasperation. The divorce was two years ago. He's past the anger phase. He's just explaining. Zoe is visible in the background, seemingly content. Jerry (as judge) is visible in elevated position if Concept A courtroom is used.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Noah. Resigned. Flat. "We now have shared custardy." He says it like reading from a filing. He made peace with this. The pun hasn't occurred to him.

**Concept A — "The Courtroom Split"**
Style: Courtroom divorce hearing, played straight, absurd ruling delivered with procedural solemnity
Duration: 9.8s — Setup specificity (pudding shop) needs room before the pun lands.

Master video prompt:
"Cartoon courtroom, vertical 9:16, 2.5D illustration, clean bold lines. Jerry Gold (brown hair, handlebar mustache, teal jacket under black judicial robes) at elevated judge's bench with gavel. Noah Check (sturdy build, short cropped hair, rectangular glasses, rumpled business casual — no ring) at the defendant's table looking resigned. Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta two-tone hair, teal glasses, teal jacket) at the opposing table looking content. Between them on the courtroom floor: a comically large ceramic bowl of yellow custard pudding with a spoon sticking out. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Courtroom
A cartoon courtroom with wooden paneling and fluorescent lighting. At the elevated judge's bench sits Jerry Gold — a middle-aged man with brown hair, handlebar mustache, wearing his teal jacket visible under black judicial robes. He holds a wooden gavel and looks down at the proceedings with the absolute seriousness of a man who believes every ruling he issues is historic. Below him at separate wooden tables: on the left, Noah Check — a sturdy man with short cropped hair, rectangular glasses, wearing a slightly rumpled button-down shirt with rolled sleeves and no tie. He sits with one elbow on the table, chin in hand, the posture of exhausted acceptance. On the right, Zoe Sparks — a young woman with orange-magenta two-tone hair, round teal glasses, teal jacket with a star pin. She sits upright, hands folded, looking serene — thriving, even. On the floor between the two tables sits a comically large ceramic bowl of bright yellow custard pudding, a large spoon sticking straight up out of it. A small card taped to the bowl reads "EXHIBIT A." Nobody acknowledges that this is unusual.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.8s): SETUP — The Ruling
Same courtroom. Jerry Gold at the bench (brown hair, mustache, teal jacket under robes) reads from a document with one hand, the other resting on his gavel. His brow is furrowed with judicial concentration. He is proud of this ruling. Noah Check at his table (short hair, glasses, rumpled button-down) has slumped further — both elbows on the table now, hands clasped in front of his mouth. He stares at the custard exhibit on the floor with the expression of a man processing paperwork for his own funeral. Across the aisle, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses) nods along with small encouraging nods, as if the divorce proceedings are going quite well. She has a small potted plant on her table that she brought from home. The custard bowl between them gleams under the fluorescent lights.
Caption: "My wife and I divorced, and the judge made us split everything evenly — including our pudding shop."

Scene 3 (5.8–6.3s): THE PAUSE
Closer framing on Noah Check at his table. Short cropped hair, rectangular glasses, rumpled shirt. He looks directly at the viewer. His expression is flat resignation — not anger, not sadness, just a man who has processed this and filed it. The custard bowl is visible at the bottom of frame. He's about to explain the arrangement. He will do so the way he would explain a home insurance claim.

Scene 4 (6.3–9.8s): PUNCHLINE — The Arrangement
Same courtroom. Noah Check at his table (short hair, glasses, rumpled shirt) looks at the viewer with complete bureaucratic acceptance. He has said it. It's filed. Behind him, Jerry Gold at the bench (mustache, robes) has set down his gavel with both hands flat on the bench — the ruling is final and he is satisfied with his work. He may have a faint smile. Across the aisle, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses) has both hands clasped together near her face, beaming — she finds the arrangement genuinely charming. "Shared custardy" delights her. The large custard bowl between the tables has been visually split — a neat line drawn through the middle of the pudding, one half tilting toward Noah's table, the other toward Zoe's. A second spoon has appeared in the bowl. Everything is procedurally correct.
Caption: "We now have shared custardy."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.8s setup | 6.3–9.8s punchline
Voice: Noah mild exasperation, reading from a filing. 0.5s pause. Punchline flat, resigned, procedural. Jerry's ruling voice is implied, not heard.
Risk: LOW. Fallback: Noah at a kitchen table with a pudding cup and a legal document, explaining his settlement.
Jerry hook: "Three cast members, courtroom drama, a giant pudding exhibit, and Jerry as the judge who is proud of the phrase 'shared custardy.' Noah's resignation is the counterweight that makes Zoe's delight land."

**Concept B — "The Pudding Shop"**
Style: Two ex-spouses operating a divided pudding shop, sitcom energy
Duration: 9.2s — Punchy commercial/domestic scene.

Master video prompt:
"Colorful pudding shop interior divided down the middle by tape, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Noah Check (short hair, glasses, rumpled apron) on the left half. Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, neat apron) on the right half. Each has their own register and display case. Sign reads 'PUDDING PALACE' — sawed in half. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.2s): HOOK — The Divided Shop
A bright, cheerful pudding shop interior with pastel walls and warm lighting. Down the exact center of the tiled floor runs a thick strip of blue painter's tape, dividing the shop in half. On the left stands Noah Check — a sturdy man with short cropped hair, rectangular glasses, wearing a slightly rumpled white apron over his button-down shirt. He leans against his counter with arms crossed, looking at his half of the shop with the resigned acceptance of a man who has measured his side to the centimeter and confirmed it is exactly equal. His display case has puddings arranged in neat but joyless rows. On the right stands Zoe Sparks — a young woman with orange-magenta two-tone hair in a ponytail, teal glasses, wearing a crisp white apron over her teal jacket. She cheerfully arranges colorful pudding cups in her display case, which has fairy lights and a small chalkboard menu with hand-drawn hearts. Her side has a potted plant. Noah's side does not. Above them, the shop sign reads "PUDDING PALACE" — but the sign has been literally sawed in half, each piece hanging at a slightly different angle. The tape line runs through counter, floor, and ceiling.

Scene 2 (2.2–5.6s): SETUP — The Situation
Same divided pudding shop. Noah Check on his side (short hair, glasses, apron) faces the viewer, one hand gesturing behind him at the divided shop. His expression is weary, explanatory — he has told this story before and he will tell it again. A pudding cup sits in his other hand. Behind the tape line on her side, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta ponytail, teal glasses, apron) serves a blurred customer with a bright smile and animated hand gestures. Her cash register has a small sign that reads "ZOE'S HALF." Noah's register has no sign — he didn't see the point. The blue tape line is prominent between them. Warm shop lighting — slightly warmer on Zoe's side.
Caption: "My wife and I divorced, and the judge made us split everything evenly — including our pudding shop."

Scene 3 (5.6–6.0s): THE BEAT
Closer framing on Noah Check at his counter. Glasses, apron, pudding cup in hand. He sighs — a visible exhale, shoulders dropping. The tape line is at the edge of frame. He is about to name this arrangement.

Scene 4 (6.0–9.2s): PUNCHLINE — The Name
Same divided pudding shop. Noah Check (glasses, apron) looks at the viewer with deadpan acceptance, holding the pudding cup slightly raised — a tired toast to his new reality. Through the tape divide, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta ponytail, teal glasses, apron) has turned toward Noah. She is grinning, both hands clasped near her chin, delighted by the whole arrangement. She catches his eye and gives him a little wave. Noah does not wave back. Above them, the split "PUDDING PALACE" sign hangs crookedly. Noah's half of the shop is in slightly cooler lighting. Zoe's half is warmer and has the fairy lights twinkling. The visual metaphor is obvious and deeply unfair to Noah.
Caption: "We now have shared custardy."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.2s no text | 2.2–5.6s setup | 6.0–9.2s punchline
Voice: Noah weary, resigned. 0.5s pause. Punchline flat, stating fact. The word "custardy" is pronounced exactly like "custody" — that's the point.
Risk: LOW. Single location, clear visual gag. Fallback: Noah and Zoe at opposite ends of a kitchen table with pudding cups between them, tape line down the middle.
Jerry hook: "The divided shop is immediately funny before anyone speaks. Zoe thriving on her side while Noah endures his side is visual storytelling that works even on mute. The sign sawed in half is the cherry."
Vince: 9:16, 9.2s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline+Zoe wave, Scene 1 divided shop reveal], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.2s]

**Concept C [SURREAL] — "The Custard Gavel"**
Style: Courtroom that transforms into pudding. Judge Jerry is proud of what he's done.
Duration: 10.0s

surreal_element: Jerry's gavel strike cracks the judge's bench, which turns out to be a giant custard bowl. Pudding floods the courtroom. Noah stands in it, resigned. Zoe scoops it with her hands.
Weirdness budget: 7/10

Master video prompt:
"Courtroom transforming into pudding, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Jerry Gold in judge robes wielding a giant spoon as gavel. Noah Check and Zoe Sparks standing waist-deep in yellow custard. Everyone treats this as normal procedure."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.5s): HOOK — Normal Courtroom (Grounded)
A normal cartoon courtroom — wooden paneling, fluorescent lights, American flag in the corner. Jerry Gold sits at the judge's bench — brown hair, handlebar mustache, teal jacket under black judicial robes. He holds a standard wooden gavel and surveys his courtroom with proprietary pride. Below at separate tables: Noah Check (short hair, glasses, rumpled button-down) sits at the left table, arms crossed, staring at the ceiling. Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, teal jacket) at the right table, hands folded, a small smile. Everything is ordinary. Professional. But on Jerry's bench, next to his papers, sits a single pudding cup with a spoon in it. Nobody has acknowledged it.

Scene 2 (2.5–5.5s): SETUP — The Ruling
Same courtroom, but reality is softening. The wooden judge's bench has begun to warp — its edges rounding, its surface taking on a ceramic glaze, turning pale yellow. It is becoming a giant custard bowl. Jerry Gold (mustache, robes) still sits in it, reading his ruling from a document that has become slightly sticky, a spot of yellow on the corner. His wooden gavel has become a large silver serving spoon. He holds it naturally — he has not noticed the change, or he does not care, because the ruling is what matters. Noah Check at his table (glasses, rumpled shirt) has noticed the custard seeping from under the bench but has chosen not to comment. His shoes are slightly wet. Zoe Sparks at her table (teal glasses, teal jacket) hasn't noticed anything — she's reading a small book titled "Starting Over: A Guide to Thriving." The floor near the bench has a thin layer of yellow custard spreading outward.
Caption: "My wife and I divorced, and the judge made us split everything evenly — including our pudding shop."

Scene 3 (5.5–6.5s): TRANSFORMATION BEAT
The courtroom has fully transformed. Jerry Gold stands inside what is now a building-sized ceramic custard bowl — the judge's bench is gone and this bowl is all that remains of it. He stands waist-deep in thick yellow pudding, judicial robes floating on the surface, holding his spoon-gavel high above his head with both hands. His expression is concentrated authority — he is about to deliver the most important strike of his career. He brings it down into the custard in a dramatic arc. CRACK — the bowl splits down the middle, a clean line from rim to rim. Yellow custard floods outward in a wave across the courtroom floor. Noah Check at his table braces, one hand gripping the table edge as custard hits his legs. His expression is the face of a man who expected this. Zoe Sparks at her table spreads her arms wide as the custard wave approaches her — welcoming it, laughing. The courtroom walls begin dripping yellow.

Scene 4 (6.5–10.0s): PUNCHLINE — Shared Custardy
The courtroom is flooded. Thick yellow custard reaches everyone's waists. The cracked bowl-bench has split into two halves — one tilting toward Noah's side, one toward Zoe's, perfectly even. Noah Check (glasses, rumpled shirt) stands in waist-deep custard, looking at the viewer with flat resignation. A pudding cup floats past him. His expression says: I signed the paperwork for this. On the other side, Zoe Sparks (teal glasses, ponytail) scoops custard with both bare hands and tastes it, genuinely happy, pudding on her chin. Between them in the background, Jerry Gold stands in the cracked bowl — robes soaked, spoon-gavel resting on his shoulder — looking down at his work with profound satisfaction. He is proud. He did this. The ruling was good. Yellow pudding covers every surface. The American flag in the corner drips. Everyone is calm.
Caption: "We now have shared custardy."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.5s no text | 2.5–5.5s setup | 6.5–10.0s punchline
Transformation beat: 5.5–6.5s — Jerry's spoon-gavel cracks the bowl-bench, pudding floods the courtroom.
Voice: Noah resigned. Jerry authoritative (implied only). 0.5s pause. Punchline flat bureaucratic. The chaos is not acknowledged.
Risk: MEDIUM. Fluid dynamics + furniture transformation. Fallback: hard cut from normal courtroom to fully-flooded courtroom — skip the morph.
Jerry hook: "This one's weird on purpose. The AI will make it weirder. That's the bit. Jerry as the judge who proudly caused a custard flood is an image that will haunt the audience. Noah standing in pudding with the expression of a man who has accepted his filing status is the second image."

- selected_concept: B
- decision: GREENLIGHT
- confidence: High
- rationale: Concept B wins because the divided pudding shop is funny before anyone speaks. A shop split down the middle by blue tape with one half thriving and one half dim is a visual joke that survives mute playback, thumbnail, and screenshot. Concept A (courtroom) takes too long to establish the pudding exhibit and dilutes the setup. Concept C (custard flood) is medium-risk production complexity for a punchline that Concept B already nails without the chaos. Noah resigned in dim lighting while Zoe's side has fairy lights is the image. That's the video.
- cost_note: Low-moderate. Single interior, two characters, tape-line prop. AI-gen friendly. The sawed sign is the only high-render-effort element — fallback is just two halves of a handwritten sign.
- must_keep: PUDDING PALACE sign sawed in half; Zoe's side visibly warmer and better decorated; Noah's flat resigned expression; Zoe's delighted wave at punchline

- next_owner: Vince
